quote: I'm just calling because we got that solar estimate built for your home. Just haven't been able to get it out to you yet. So I just need to find a good time for our specialist to bring that over.
stewart_read: Carter opens with a fabricated premise — an estimate that was 'already built' but 'hasn't been sent yet' — rather than either the Tesla intro or the standard form-fill intro. This is not a strategic choice; there is no later move it sets up. It is a reactive improvisation that skips legitimacy framing entirely and manufactures false urgency. The customer never pushes back on the premise, but he also never engages with it — he moves straight to scheduling logistics.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is using the 'estimate already built' framing as a curiosity hook to lower resistance. Rejected — the codex's curiosity_over_features principle requires creating genuine curiosity about the customer's specific math, not a fabricated deliverable. This framing creates a promise Carter cannot keep and a premise the closer will have to walk back.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the Tesla intro template and the standard intro template side by side. Ask him what legitimacy signal he gave John in the first 30 seconds. The answer is none. The fabricated-estimate opener is a liability: if John asks to see the estimate before the appointment, Carter has no answer. Drill the intro until legitimacy-first is the default.

quote: And then a couple of things I need to check first, though. So there's a huge tax incentive. You You probably heard that. You just need to pay income tax and have a credit score above six fifty. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Carter jumps from the fabricated opener directly to the credit qualifier, skipping every verify beat: no address confirmation, no title/co-owners, no interest_in_solar, no utility company, no average bill, no roof type, no prior designs. The call_shape requires verify before qualifiers. Carter also states the credit threshold as 650, not 670 — a schema drift flagged in qualifiers.credit_qualifier.drift_observed. The verify section is not a formality; it is where bill pain is anchored and spouse/co-owner status is captured. Skipping it means Carter has no emotional leverage and no knowledge of whether a co-decision-maker exists.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is running a compressed call because John signaled he was busy. Rejected — John had not yet signaled busyness at 01:05. The skip is structural, not situational.
coaching_implication: This is the single largest structural gap on the call. Walk Carter through call_shape.required_beats and ask him to identify which beats he hit. He will find he hit one (credit qualifier) out of six required. The manager should use this call as a drill anchor: run Carter through a mock call where he cannot advance to the qualifier until he has completed at least 6 of 8 verify items. Also flag the 650 vs 670 threshold drift for reconciliation with Kenny.

quote: Which, we have two houses. Which house did you do it for? The 26, 170 or the 26474?
stewart_read: John volunteers that he owns two properties. This is a material lead-expansion signal: a second property is a second potential solar install, a second roofing opportunity, and a second sit. Carter's only response is to confirm which address the 'estimate' was built for — he does not acknowledge the second property, does not ask anything about it, and moves on. The signal is walked past entirely.
coaching_implication: Ask Carter: 'What did you do when John told you he had two houses?' The answer is nothing. Coach the move: acknowledge the second property explicitly ('Wait — you have two homes? Is the other one also yours?') and flag it for the closer. Even a single sentence plants the seed. This is a cross-sell signal that costs nothing to capture and could double the deal value.

quote: I'm I'm real busy right now. Oh, I see it. I can't do it on Tuesday. Sorry. I have an appointment then.
stewart_read: John's 'real busy' signal arrives with zero rapport buffer. Carter had no bill pain on the table, no curiosity created, no emotional alignment built. When scheduling friction hit, there was nothing holding John in the conversation. This is the downstream consequence of skipping the verify section — the call had no anchor to return to when John started pulling away.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Carter what bill anchoring is for. If Carter had asked about John's average bill at minute one and reflected it back ('So you're paying around $X a month — that's what the design is built to address'), John's 'I'm busy' would have had something to push against. Without the anchor, the call had no gravity. This is a teaching moment for why verify comes before scheduling.

quote: My scheduler is not here right now, and my computer is not working.
stewart_read: John offers a computer-failure excuse that functions as a soft exit. Carter accepts it immediately and pivots to 'give me a call tomorrow after 3 PM' — but does not lock that as a confirmed callback appointment. Per analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time is not a sit-eligible outcome. Carter then further softens it by offering to text instead, which John accepts. The result is a passive follow-up with no commitment structure.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is preserving the relationship by not pushing a resistant customer, planning to lock the time via text. Rejected — Carter never locks a specific time in the text offer either. The offer is open-ended ('once you know when you're available, just let me know'), which is the definition of a soft callback with no anchor.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the button_up protocol. Even in a callback scenario, the move is to lock a specific time: 'I'll text you — and let's say I'll reach back out at 3:15 tomorrow. Does that work?' That converts a passive 'text me' into a confirmed callback with a time anchor. Walk Carter through the difference between a locked callback and an open-ended follow-up, and what each one means for his sit rate.

quote: Yeah. We can give you a callback to set something up. Or would it be best if I just texted you?
stewart_read: Carter offers two options — callback or text — without anchoring either to a specific time. This is the third scheduling-friction touch (per multi_touch_patterns) and the weakest handling of the three. The 'or would it be best if I just texted you?' framing hands John full control of the follow-up timeline, which is the opposite of what button_up requires. No verbal handshake, no 'is that fair,' no time block stated.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter is reading John's urgency ('I gotta go') and choosing not to push, preserving goodwill for the text follow-up. Partially valid — pushing hard on a customer who has said 'I gotta go' twice would likely end the call worse. But the strategic move here is to lock a specific text-back time, not to leave the timeline open. The goodwill preservation is real; the time-anchor omission is still a miss.
coaching_implication: This is the moment where a single sentence recovers the sit: 'I'll text you right now so you have my number — and I'll follow up at 3:15 tomorrow once your scheduler is back. Sound good?' That's a locked callback disguised as a text. Coach Carter on the difference between accommodating a customer's pace and surrendering the time anchor entirely.

quote: Does that sound good? I gotta go. I yeah. I gotta go. Yeah. Do that. Thank you. Yep. Take care.
stewart_read: Carter's 'Does that sound good?' arrives as John is already mid-exit. The verbal handshake — the one element of button_up that predicts show rate — lands on a customer who has said 'I gotta go' twice. There is no confirmation, no 'is that fair,' no appointment details restated. The call ends with John's 'Take care' as a social close, not a commitment close.
coaching_implication: The 'is that fair' handshake only works if it comes before the customer is already out the door. This moment illustrates why the verify section and bill anchor matter upstream — they create the engagement that makes the button_up land. Carter is trying to use button_up mechanics on a call that never built the foundation button_up requires. Use this as a sequencing lesson, not just a button_up lesson.

quote: So there's a huge tax incentive. You You probably heard that. You just need to pay income tax and have a credit score above six fifty.
stewart_read: Carter states the credit threshold as 650, not 670. This matches the drift observed in SESSION18_fcbf33c1 and flagged in the codex. Whether 650 is the current active threshold or a rep error is unresolved — but Carter is either working from outdated information or improvising. Either way, the threshold stated to the customer does not match the schema.
coaching_implication: Flag for Kenny: is the active threshold 650 or 670? Until resolved, Carter (and any rep stating 650) may be qualifying customers the closer will DQ, or DQ'ing customers the closer would have passed. This is an operational truth gap, not a coaching gap — but the manager should surface it to Kenny before coaching Carter on the number.
